data: Each browser/OS image is a self-contained Docker image stored in ECR. When a session is launched, ECS pulls the image and runs it as a Fargate task.
data.image-structure#Image Structure:
Each image lives in docker/vbrowsers/{name}/:
docker/vbrowsers/chrome/
├── Dockerfile       # Image definition
└── 1-app.sh         # Container startup script
1-app.sh runs inside the container on startup. It:
Creates a dynamic Cloudflare DNS A record pointing to the container's public IP
Starts Selkies — the VNC-over-websockets server that streams the browser to the user's browser
Cloudflare credentials (CF_API_TOKEN, CF_ZONE_ID) and CUSTOM_DOMAIN are placeholder values in source control. Real values are injected at build time by build_browsers.sh.
data.building-images#Building Images:
docker/build_browsers.sh builds and pushes images to ECR. It is called automatically by setup.sh during initial provisioning.To build and push images manually:
cd docker
./build_browsers.sh
The script:
Authenticates with ECR using your AWS credentials
For each selected image in terraform.tfvars:
Builds the image with real Cloudflare and domain values injected as build arguments
Tags and pushes it to ECR
data.adding-a-custom-image#Adding a Custom Im:
All vBrowser images are built on top of the vbrowser/selkies-baseimage:ubuntu base image, which itself is derived from the LinuxServer.io selkies baseimage. Credit to the LinuxServer.io team for their excellent work on the base infrastructure.Your Dockerfile should start with:
FROM vbrowser/selkies-baseimage:ubuntu
For a detailed reference on how the base image works and how to extend it, see the linuxserver/docker-baseimage-selkies repository.To add a new browser image:
Create a directory at docker/vbrowsers/{name}/
Add a Dockerfile starting with FROM vbrowser/selkies-baseimage:ubuntu — see the baseimage reference or an existing image (e.g. chrome) for guidance
Add a 1-app.sh startup script using CF_API_TOKEN, CF_ZONE_ID, and CUSTOM_DOMAIN as build-time placeholders
Add the image name to docker_images in terraform/terraform.tfvars
Run terraform apply to create the ECS task definition
Run build_browsers.sh to build and push the image
data.updating-an-existing-image#Updating an E:
After modifying a browser's Dockerfile or 1-app.sh:
cd docker
./build_browsers.sh
Then redeploy to pick up the new image on the next session launch. Running ECS tasks continue using the old image until they stop.

data: OvB supports 15 browser and OS images. Each runs as an isolated ECS Fargate container and streams to you via Selkies over HTTPS.
data.feature-availability#Feature Availabilit:
Not all features are available for all images:
Feature	Unsupported images	Network traffic logging	Kali, Tor, Telegram	File protection	Kali
data.resource-allocation#Resource Allocation:
Browser and OS images use separate resource allocations configured in SiteSettings:
Browser images — browser_vcpu and browser_memory_gb
OS images (Kali) — os_vcpu and os_memory_gb
OS images use higher defaults because they run a full desktop environment.
data.selecting-images-at-setup#Selecting Imag: During setup.sh, you are prompted to select which images to install. Your selection is written to terraform/terraform.tfvars under docker_images. Only selected images will have ECS task definitions created and Docker images built and pushed to ECR.To add images later, update docker_images in terraform.tfvars and re-run terraform apply, then rebuild with build_browsers.sh.

data: OvB is deployed with a single interactive setup script that provisions all AWS infrastructure via Terraform, builds your selected browser images, and starts the application stack with Docker Compose.
data.what-you-need#What You Need:
Before running the setup script you must have the following ready:
Requirement	Purpose	Linux host (amd64)	Running the Docker Compose stack	Terraform	Provisioning AWS infrastructure	Docker + Docker Compose	Running the application	AWS account with IAM credentials	ECS Fargate, ECR, VPC, IAM	Domain on Cloudflare	Per-session DNS A records
data.supported-platforms#Supported Platforms:
Architecture	Platform	Supported	Notes	amd64	Linux	Yes	Fully tested	amd64	Windows	Likely	Untested	aarch64 / arm64	Linux	Likely	Untested	
All documentation assumes Linux (amd64).
data.setup-flow#Setup Flow:
Install prerequisites — Terraform, AWS credentials, Cloudflare
Clone the repository
Export AWS credentials
Run ./terraform/setup.sh — interactive; prompts for all remaining config
See the Quickstart for the complete step-by-step walkthrough.

data.terraform#Terraform:
OvB uses Terraform to provision AWS infrastructure. Install it on a Debian/Ubuntu system:1. Update and install prerequisites
sudo apt-get update -y && sudo apt-get install -y gnupg software-properties-common
2. Install the HashiCorp GPG key
wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | \
gpg --dearmor | \
sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg > /dev/null
3. Add the HashiCorp repository
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(grep -oP '(?<=UBUNTU_CODENAME=).*' /etc/os-release || lsb_release -cs) main" | \
sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
4. Install Terraform
sudo apt update -y && sudo apt-get install -y terraform
For other operating systems, see the official Terraform install guide.
data.aws-credentials#AWS Credentials:
Terraform needs AWS credentials to provision ECS, ECR, VPC, and IAM resources.1. Sign in to AWSGo to https://aws.amazon.com/console/. Using an IAM user (not root) with the required permissions is recommended.The IAM user needs permissions for: ECS, ECR, EC2 (VPC/subnets/security groups), IAM, CloudWatch Logs, and S3.2. Create access keys
Navigate to IAM → Users → your user → Security credentials
Click Create access key
Copy both values:
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
You will only see the secret key once. Store it securely.
3. Export credentials
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="your-access-key-id"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="your-secret-access-key"
The AWS region is set interactively when you run setup.sh — no manual configuration needed.
data.cloudflare#Cloudflare:
OvB uses Cloudflare to create a unique DNS A record for each browser session (browser-{hash}.yourdomain.com → container public IP).1. Create a Cloudflare accountSign up at https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up. The free tier is sufficient.2. Add your domainAdd your domain to Cloudflare and update your registrar's nameservers to the ones Cloudflare assigns.3. Copy your Zone IDOpen your domain's Overview page in the Cloudflare dashboard. The Zone ID is in the bottom-right panel. Copy it.4. Create an API token
Go to https://dash.cloudflare.com/profile/api-tokens
Click Create Token → select the Edit zone DNS template
Set permissions: Zone.DNS: Edit, scoped to your specific zone
Copy the token — you will only see it once
Do not use the Global API Key. It grants full account access.
data.docker#Docker:
Docker and Docker Compose must be installed on the host running the stack.
# Install Docker Engine
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
# Verify
docker --version
docker compose version

data: This guide walks through a first-time deployment from clone to a running OvB instance.
data.2-export-aws-credentials#2. Export AWS C:
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="your-access-key-id"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="your-secret-access-key"
These are used by Terraform to provision AWS infrastructure. See AWS Credentials if you haven't created them yet.
data.3-run-the-setup-script#3. Run the Setup:
cd terraform
./setup.sh
setup.sh is fully interactive — it will prompt you for everything else. Have the following ready:
Prompt	Description	Browser/OS images	Which images to install (or select all)	Domain	Your Cloudflare-managed domain (e.g. example.com)	AWS region	e.g. us-east-1	Cloudflare Zone ID	From your domain's Overview page	Cloudflare API token	From your API Tokens page	Database credentials	Username and password for PostgreSQL	Django admin email	Superuser login for the admin panel	Django admin password	Superuser password
data.what-happens-next#What Happens Next:
setup.sh performs all provisioning automatically:
Updates terraform.tfvars with your selected browser images
Writes all config to docker/.env
Runs terraform init and terraform apply — provisions VPC, subnets, security groups, ECR, ECS cluster, IAM roles, and CloudWatch log groups
Terraform appends ECR registry URL, subnet ID, security group ID, and AWS credentials back to docker/.env
Builds and pushes selected browser images to ECR via build_browsers.sh
Builds the backend and frontend Docker images
Starts all services with docker compose up -d
data.after-setup#After Setup:
Once the stack is running, open your browser and navigate to https://yourdomain.com. Log in with the Django admin credentials you provided during setup.
setup.sh is for first-time provisioning only. For code updates, see Redeploying Changes.
data.next-steps#Next Steps:
Reverse proxy options — switch to Nginx or Caddy if you prefer
Supported browsers — understand the available images
Launching a session — start your first browser session

data.redeploying-code-changes#Redeploying Cod:
After modifying backend or frontend code, rebuild and restart the stack. Use whichever compose file you are currently running:
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml build --no-cache
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml up -d --force-recreate
docker compose restart does not re-read .env. Always use --force-recreate when environment variables have changed.
If you are only rebuilding specific services, you can pass the service name:
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml build --no-cache ovb_backend
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml up -d --force-recreate ovb_backend
data.adding-or-removing-browser-images#Adding:
To add or remove images from your deployment:
Edit terraform/terraform.tfvars — update the docker_images list
Run terraform apply to update ECS task definitions:
cd terraform
terraform apply
Run build_browsers.sh for newly added images:
cd docker
./build_browsers.sh
data.environment-variable-changes#Environment:
If you need to update secrets or configuration in docker/.env:
Edit docker/.env directly
Restart the affected services with --force-recreate:
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml up -d --force-recreate
data.destroying-the-deployment#Destroying the:
To tear everything down — all AWS resources, ECS tasks, ECR images, and local containers:
cd terraform
./destroy.sh
This runs terraform destroy to remove all Terraform-managed AWS infrastructure and stops all Docker containers.
Any active browser sessions will be terminated immediately. Stored data in PostgreSQL (sessions, cases, notes) will be lost unless you have backed up the database volume.
To redeploy from scratch after destroying, run ./setup.sh again.

data:
OvB provides three reverse proxy options. All are in the docker/ directory as separate compose files.
Compose file	Reverse proxy	TLS method	docker-compose.traefik.yml	Traefik	Cloudflare DNS challenge (automatic)	docker-compose.nginx.yml	Nginx + Certbot	Let's Encrypt HTTP challenge	docker-compose.caddy.yml	Caddy	Let's Encrypt HTTP challenge (automatic)	docker-compose.yml	None	Used by setup.sh for initial provisioning only
data.traefik-recommended#Traefik (Recommended:
Traefik uses the Cloudflare DNS challenge to automatically obtain and renew TLS certificates. No manual certificate management is required.Requirements: CF_Zone_ID and CF_Token must be set in docker/.env (they are written there by setup.sh).
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml up -d
Traefik handles certificate issuance and renewal automatically on startup.
data.nginx--certbot#Nginx + Certbot:
Nginx requires a two-step startup: first obtain the certificate over HTTP, then enable HTTPS.1. Configure the domainEdit docker/nginx/nginx.conf and replace every occurrence of OVB_DOMAIN with your domain.2. Add the Certbot emailAdd CERTBOT_EMAIL=your@email.com to docker/.env.3. Start in HTTP-only modeThe HTTPS server { } block in nginx.conf is commented out by default. Start the stack first:
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.nginx.yml up -d
4. Obtain the initial certificate
docker compose -f docker-compose.nginx.yml run --rm ovb_certbot
5. Enable HTTPSUncomment the HTTPS server { } block in docker/nginx/nginx.conf, then reload:
docker compose -f docker-compose.nginx.yml exec ovb_nginx nginx -s reload
Renewing certificates
docker compose -f docker-compose.nginx.yml run --rm ovb_certbot renew
docker compose -f docker-compose.nginx.yml exec ovb_nginx nginx -s reload
data.caddy#Caddy:
Caddy automatically obtains and renews certificates via Let's Encrypt.1. Configure the domainEdit docker/Caddyfile and replace example.com with your domain.2. Start the stack
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.caddy.yml up -d
Caddy handles everything else automatically.
data.switching-reverse-proxies#Switching Reve:
To switch from one proxy to another, stop the current stack and start with the new compose file:
cd docker
docker compose -f docker-compose.traefik.yml down
docker compose -f docker-compose.caddy.yml up -d

data:
terraform/setup.sh is the first-time provisioning entrypoint. It handles everything from infrastructure provisioning to starting the application stack.
Run setup.sh only once for initial setup. For subsequent code changes, use the redeployment workflow.
data.step-1--select-browser-images#Step 1 — S: The script prompts you to choose which browser/OS images to install. You can select individual images or install all 15. Your selection is written to terraform/terraform.tfvars under docker_images.Only selected images will have ECS task definitions created and will be pushed to ECR.
data.step-2--collect-configuration#Step 2 — C:
The script interactively collects:
Input	Written to	Domain	docker/.env as CUSTOM_DOMAIN	AWS region	docker/.env and terraform.tfvars as aws_region	Cloudflare Zone ID	docker/.env as CF_Zone_ID	Cloudflare API token	docker/.env as CF_Token	Database name, user, password	docker/.env as DB_*	Redis URL	docker/.env as REDIS_URL	Django superuser email + password	docker/.env as DJANGO_SUPERUSER_*
data.step-3--terraform-apply#Step 3 — Terrafo:
Runs terraform init and terraform apply -auto-approve to provision:
VPC, public subnets, security groups
ECR repository
ECS cluster
IAM roles and user
CloudWatch log groups
ECS task definitions for selected browser images
After apply, Terraform appends ECR_REGISTRY, SUBNET_ID, SECURITY_GROUP_ID, AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY to docker/.env.
data.step-4--build-and-push-browser-images#St:
Runs docker/build_browsers.sh for each selected image. This:
Builds the browser Docker image from docker/vbrowsers/{name}/
Injects Cloudflare credentials and the domain into the image at build time
Pushes the image to ECR
data.step-5--build-application-images#Step 5: Runs docker compose build --no-cache using docker/docker-compose.yml to build the Django backend and Next.js frontend images.
data.step-6--start-the-stack#Step 6 — Start t:
Runs docker compose up -d to start all services:
ovb_backend — Django (Gunicorn + Daphne/ASGI)
ovb_frontend — Next.js
ovb_db — PostgreSQL 16
ovb_redis — Redis
ovb_celery — Celery worker
ovb_celery_beat — Celery beat scheduler
data.secrets-and-config-files#Secrets and Con:
File	Status	Description	docker/.env	Gitignored	Written by Terraform; contains all secrets	terraform/terraform.tfvars	Gitignored	Infrastructure config	terraform/terraform.tfstate	Gitignored	Terraform state; contains plaintext credentials	backend/.env	Gitignored	Copy from backend/example.env if needed	frontend/.env.local	Gitignored	Copy from frontend/example.env.local if needed	
Never commit terraform.tfvars, terraform.tfstate, or docker/.env. These contain plaintext credentials.

data: Open vBrowser is a self-hosted browser isolation platform built for cyber threat intelligence investigations, security operations, and anyone who needs to browse the internet without exposing their identity or infrastructure.Each session runs in a fully ephemeral, isolated container on AWS ECS Fargate. The container is destroyed when the session ends — nothing persists between sessions. A unique Cloudflare DNS subdomain is created on the fly for each session so the browser streams directly to you over HTTPS.
data.why-ovb#Why OvB?:
Many SOC teams and CTI analysts lack access to sandboxed browsing environments for dark/deep web investigations. Commercial alternatives are expensive and lock useful features behind paywalls. OvB provides this infrastructure at a minimal cost with full self-hosted control.Key capabilities:
15 browser and OS images — Chrome, Firefox, Tor, Brave, Kali Linux, and more
Zero persistence — containers are ephemeral; no cross-session state
Network traffic logging — every HTTP request captured and logged per session
File protection — downloaded files are encrypted before leaving the container
Screenshots — capture the browser viewport at any point and download from session history
Case management — organize sessions into investigation cases with notes and tags
Multi-workspace support — team and personal workspaces with role-based access
MFA enforcement — TOTP second factor required on all API calls
OIDC/SSO — optional single sign-on via Google, Okta, Azure AD, or any OIDC provider
Cost tracking — per-session Fargate compute cost estimates
AWS Spot support — optional Fargate Spot for lower costs
data.status#Status: OvB is v1.0. It has been tested on Linux (amd64). Other platforms may work but are not officially supported.
data.quick-links#Quick Links:
Prerequisites — Terraform, AWS, Cloudflare
Quickstart — Clone, configure, and launch
Supported Browsers — Full image catalog
GitHub Repository
data.legal#Legal: By using vBrowser you agree to comply with all applicable laws and accept sole responsibility for your actions within the platform. The authors and contributors of vBrowser are not liable for any damages arising from use or misuse of the software.See the Liability & Acceptable Use page for full terms, and the License for licensing details.

data: Open vBrowser is licensed under a custom Non-Commercial License.
data.summary#Summary: Use case	Permitted	Personal use	Yes	Educational use	Yes	Internal use within an organization (non-commercial)	Yes	Commercial use	No — requires written permission	Resale or redistribution as a paid service	No — requires written permission	Modification	Yes, for permitted use cases
data.full-license-text#Full License Text:
You may use, modify, and distribute the software for personal and educational use only.Commercial use and resale are strictly prohibited without express written permission from the author.
The full license text is included in the LICENSE file in the repository.
data.contact#Contact: For commercial licensing inquiries, contact the author through the GitHub repository.
data.copyright#Copyright: Copyright © Joseph Fisher. All rights reserved.

data.compliance-with-laws#Compliance with Law: By using vBrowser, you agree to comply with all applicable local, state, national, and international laws and regulations. Any illegal activity — including but not limited to hacking, fraud, and distribution of illegal content — is strictly prohibited and may result in immediate suspension of your account and reporting to the appropriate authorities.
data.user-responsibility#User Responsibility: vBrowser is not responsible for any actions taken by users within the vBrowser environment. Users are solely responsible for their actions and any consequences arising from misuse. vBrowser reserves the right to monitor, review, and remove any content that violates these terms.
data.no-liability#No Liability: Under no circumstance shall the authors or contributors of vBrowser be liable for any damages — direct, indirect, incidental, special, exemplary, or consequential — arising from the use or misuse of the software. The software is provided as is, without warranty of any kind.
data.acknowledgement#Acknowledgement: By creating a session, you acknowledge and agree to these terms. You also acknowledge that you have read the documentation on how to operate vBrowser sessions.For full license terms, see the LICENSE file in the repository.

data.isolation-model#Isolation Model:
Every browser session in OvB runs in a fully isolated AWS ECS Fargate container. Key isolation properties:
No persistent storage — the container filesystem is ephemeral. When the session ends, the container is destroyed and nothing is written back to any shared volume.
Dedicated public IP — each container gets its own public IP via Fargate's ENI assignment. There is no shared networking between sessions.
Per-session DNS — a unique subdomain (browser-{hash}.yourdomain.com) is created for each session and deleted when it ends.
Session tokens — each container receives a unique token for self-close and traffic reporting. User cookies are never passed to the container.
data.admin-settings-sitesettings#Admin Settin: Admins configure platform-wide behavior from the Admin → Settings panel. All settings are stored in the SiteSettings singleton in the database.
data.access-control#Access Control: Setting	Description	User registration	Open or closed. When closed, only admins can create accounts.	Allow workspace creation	Whether regular users can create team workspaces	Allow personal workspaces	Whether users automatically get a personal workspace	MFA enforcement	Require TOTP on all accounts (coming)
data.browser-allowlists#Browser Allowlists: Admins can restrict which browser/OS images are available globally. Workspace owners can further restrict this per-workspace.
data.session-limits#Session Limits: Setting	Description	Max concurrent sessions per user	Global cap on simultaneously open sessions	Max workspaces per user	Cap on how many workspaces a user can own
data.resource-provisioning#Resource Provision:
ECS task resource overrides are set per image type:
Setting	Description	browser_vcpu	vCPU allocated to browser-type images	browser_memory_gb	Memory (GB) allocated to browser-type images	os_vcpu	vCPU allocated to OS-type images (e.g. Kali)	os_memory_gb	Memory (GB) allocated to OS-type images	
These are injected as ECS task-level overrides at launch time.
data.feature-flags#Feature Flags: Setting	Description	Network logging	Enable/disable globally	File protection	Enable/disable globally	Fargate Spot	Allow users to opt into Spot capacity
data.oidc--sso#OIDC / SSO: See OIDC / SSO for configuration details.
data.user-management#User Management: Admins can manage all user accounts from Admin → Users. From here you can create accounts, reset passwords, assign staff or superuser privileges, and deactivate users.
data.transport-security#Transport Security:
In production, all traffic between users and the application should go through HTTPS. Set the following environment variables in docker/.env when running behind a TLS-terminating reverse proxy:
SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT=1
SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=1
CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE=1
SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS=31536000
SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS=1
SECURE_HSTS_PRELOAD=1
The browser containers themselves stream over HTTPS via Selkies, using the Cloudflare-managed TLS certificate for your domain.

data: OvB supports TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) multi-factor authentication via django-allauth MFA. MFA is enforced on all authenticated API calls.
data.setting-up-mfa#Setting Up MFA:
Log in to your account
Navigate to Account → Security
Click Enable two-factor authentication
Scan the QR code with an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, etc.)
Enter the 6-digit code to confirm setup
Once enabled, you will be prompted for your TOTP code on every login.
data.mfa-enforcement#MFA Enforcement: MFA is enforced at the API level via the session_mfa_auth authentication backend. Every API call to protected endpoints requires the session to have completed MFA verification. If you are logged in but have not completed MFA, API calls will return a 401.
data.recovery#Recovery: If you lose access to your authenticator app, contact your administrator. Admins can disable MFA on a user's account from the Admin → Users panel.
data.admin-disabling-mfa-for-a-user#Admin: Di:
Go to Admin → Users
Find the user
Click Disable MFA
This removes their TOTP device. The user will need to re-enroll MFA on their next login.

data: OvB supports single sign-on via OpenID Connect (OIDC). Any OIDC-compatible identity provider (IdP) can be used — there is no hard dependency on a specific vendor.
data.what-you-need#What You Need:
To connect an IdP, you need three things from your provider:
Value	Description	Provider type	One of google, okta, azure, or generic	Client ID	Issued by your IdP when you register the application	Client Secret	Issued alongside the Client ID	
Once you supply these, vBrowser will give you a callback URL to register with your IdP:
https://yourdomain.com/accounts/oidc/<provider>/login/callback/
data.configuration#Configuration: OIDC is configured via environment variables in docker/.env or via the Admin Settings panel.
data.admin-settings-panel#Admin Settings pane: OIDC can also be configured from Admin → Settings → SSO without restarting the stack. Changes take effect immediately.
data.behavior#Behavior:
When OIDC is enabled:
A Sign in with <provider> button appears on the login page
New users signing in via OIDC for the first time get an account created automatically (if registration is open)
OIDC users still go through MFA enforcement if MFA is enabled on their account
data.disabling-oidc#Disabling OIDC: Remove or clear the OIDC_* environment variables and restart the stack, or disable it from the Admin Settings panel.

data: Cases let you organize browser sessions into structured investigations. Each case can contain linked sessions, notes, tags, comments, and file attachments.
data.creating-a-case#Creating a Case:
Navigate to Cases in your workspace sidebar
Click New case
Enter a name and optional description
Choose a status: open, closed, or archived
data.case-status#Case Status: Status	Meaning	open	Active investigation	closed	Investigation complete	archived	Preserved for record-keeping; no longer active
data.linking-sessions-to-a-case#Linking Sessi: Sessions can be linked to a case from the session detail view in Session History. A single session can belong to one case.
data.tags#Tags:
Tags are colored labels you can apply to sessions to categorize them (e.g. by threat actor, campaign, or topic). Tags are scoped to the workspace.To create a tag:
Go to Cases → Tags
Click New tag, enter a name, and pick a color
To apply a tag to a session, open the session in history and select tags from the detail panel.
data.session-notes#Session Notes:
Each session can have a rich-text note attached to it. Notes are written with the BlockNote editor, which supports:
Headings and paragraphs
Bullet and numbered lists
Bold, italic, and other inline formatting
Code blocks
Notes are stored per-session and are accessible from the session detail view.
data.case-comments#Case Comments: Cases have a comment thread for team collaboration. Any workspace member can add comments.
data.file-attachments#File Attachments: Files can be attached directly to cases (not individual sessions). Attachments are limited to 50 MB per file and are stored on the server's local filesystem by default. If AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME is configured, attachments are stored in S3 instead.
data.case-detail-view#Case Detail View:
The case detail page shows:
Case metadata (name, description, status, created date)
All linked sessions with summary info
Comment thread
File attachments
From here you can also change the case status or edit the description.

data: File protection encrypts files downloaded inside a browser session before they can leave the container. This prevents accidentally executing malicious files and ensures downloads are handled intentionally.
data.how-it-works#How It Works: When file protection is enabled for a session, any file downloaded inside the browser is automatically encrypted with 7-Zip AES-256 encryption inside the container. The encrypted archive (.7z) is what gets transferred to the user instead of the raw file.To access the file contents, you must decrypt it with the session-specific password using 7-Zip or a compatible tool.
data.limitations#Limitations: Constraint	Details	Maximum file size	10 MB. Files larger than 10 MB may stall or fail during encryption due to the double-processing overhead.	Kali Linux	File protection is not supported on Kali (full OS image).	File format	All protected files are delivered as .7z archives regardless of original format.
data.enabling-file-protection#Enabling File P:
Like network logging, file protection can be enabled at three levels:
Global (SiteSettings) — admin enables/disables it platform-wide
Workspace — workspace owners/admins can override the global setting
Per session — if allowed by the workspace, users can toggle it at launch time
data.decrypting-a-protected-file#Decrypting a:
You will need 7-Zip or a compatible tool. The decryption password is provided in the session interface.Linux/macOS:
7z x downloaded-file.7z
Enter the password when prompted.Windows:Right-click the .7z file → 7-Zip → Extract here, then enter the password.
data.why-use-file-protection#Why Use File Pro:
Encrypted delivery ensures:
You cannot accidentally double-click and execute a malware sample
Files from high-risk sites are contained until you consciously choose to decrypt them
There is a clear audit trail (the encrypted file) of what was retrieved during the session

data.the-application-grid#The Application Gri: After logging in, you land on the main application grid for your active workspace. This grid shows all browser and OS images available to your workspace.Click any image to open the launch panel.
data.launch-options#Launch Options:
Option	Description	Starting URL	Optional URL to open when the browser starts	Fargate Spot	Use AWS Fargate Spot capacity (cheaper, interruptible)	Network logging	Capture all HTTP traffic for this session (if enabled for your workspace)	File protection	Encrypt downloaded files inside the container (if enabled for your workspace)	
Some images may have Spot forced on or off based on their configuration. OS-type images (e.g. Kali) may have different defaults.
data.session-lifecycle#Session Lifecycle:
You click a browser image and submit the launch form
The backend creates a session record and queues an ECS task via Celery
A Celery worker calls ecs.run_task() on Fargate with the selected image
The loading page polls the session status until the container is running
Once running, Fargate assigns a public IP to the container. The backend creates a Cloudflare DNS A record: browser-{hash}.yourdomain.com
The callback updates the session with the live URL
The loading page redirects to the live session viewer
data.the-live-session-viewer#The Live Session: The session viewer embeds the browser via a Selkies iframe. The browser runs inside the isolated container and streams directly to your browser over HTTPS.The session viewer URL is unique to each session and authenticated by a per-session token — it cannot be accessed without it.
data.closing-a-session#Closing a Session:
You can close a session by:
Clicking the close/stop button in the session viewer
Navigating to Session History and stopping the session from there
The container self-closes when the idle timeout expires (default: 10 minutes of inactivity)
When a session closes, the container is stopped, the Cloudflare DNS record is deleted, and the session cost is recorded.
data.concurrent-session-limits#Concurrent Ses: Admins can configure per-user and per-workspace limits on concurrent open sessions via SiteSettings. If you hit the limit, you must close an existing session before starting a new one.

data: Network logging captures every HTTP request made from inside a browser session. This is useful for CTI investigations — you can see exactly what hosts and URLs the browser contacted without needing to analyze packet captures.
data.how-it-works#How It Works:
When network logging is enabled for a session, a mitmproxy instance runs inside the container alongside the browser. All HTTP/HTTPS traffic from the browser is routed through mitmproxy, which extracts and forwards:
HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.)
Host
Full URL
Timestamp
This data is shipped back to the backend as TrafficEvent records, associated with the session.
data.limitations#Limitations:
Network logging is not supported on the following images because they route traffic in ways that are incompatible with mitmproxy:
Image	Reason	Tor Browser	Traffic goes through Tor; mitmproxy cannot intercept	Telegram	Not a web browser	Kali Linux	Full OS; mitmproxy is not injected
data.enabling-network-logging#Enabling Networ:
Network logging can be enabled or disabled at three levels:
Global (SiteSettings) — admin enables/disables it platform-wide
Workspace — workspace owners/admins can override the global setting
Per session — if allowed by the workspace, users can toggle it at launch time
If the feature is disabled at a higher level, it cannot be enabled at a lower one.
data.viewing-traffic-logs#Viewing Traffic Log:
Go to Session History
Click on a completed or active session
Expand the Network Logs section in the detail panel
Each entry shows the timestamp, HTTP method, host, and full URL.
data.privacy-and-data-retention#Privacy and D: Traffic logs are stored in the PostgreSQL database. They are associated with the session and accessible to workspace members with sufficient permissions. There is currently no automatic expiry — logs are retained until the session record is deleted.

data: Screenshots let you capture the current state of a browser session as a PNG image without leaving the session viewer. The image is saved to your session and can be downloaded at any time from Session History.
data.taking-a-screenshot#Taking a Screenshot:
Open an active session in the session viewer
Click the Screenshot button in the session toolbar
The capture happens instantly — a confirmation appears when the screenshot is saved
The screenshot captures the full visible browser viewport at the moment you click.
data.accessing-saved-screenshots#Accessing Sa:
Screenshots are attached to the session they were taken in. To view or download them:
Go to Session History
Find the session and open its detail view
Screenshots are listed in the Screenshots tab — click any thumbnail to download
Screenshots are available for the lifetime of the session record. They are not deleted when the container stops.
data.limitations#Limitations: Constraint	Details	Format	PNG only	Scope	Captures the browser viewport — not the full page or off-screen content	Kali Linux	Screenshots work on Kali but capture the full desktop environment, not just the browser window	Storage	Screenshots are stored server-side; very large numbers of captures per session may affect performance
data.why-take-screenshots#Why Take Screenshot:
Screenshots are useful for:
Preserving evidence of what was seen during an investigation without downloading files
Documenting threat actor infrastructure, phishing pages, or suspicious content
Creating a lightweight audit trail alongside network logs

data: The Session History page shows all past and active sessions in the current workspace.
data.whats-recorded#What's Recorded:
For every session, OvB records:
Field	Description	Browser/OS image	Which image was used	Start time	When the session started	End time	When the session closed	Duration	Total session length	Status	pending, active, closed, error	Starting URL	URL passed at launch (if any)	Fargate Spot	Whether Spot capacity was used	Estimated cost	Fargate compute cost in USD	Network logs	HTTP traffic captured during the session	Tags	Any tags applied to the session	Case	Linked case (if any)	Notes	Rich-text session note (if created)
data.session-status#Session Status: Status	Meaning	pending	Container is starting; ECS task not yet running	active	Container is running and the session is live	closed	Container has stopped; session is complete	error	Something went wrong during startup or shutdown
data.stopping-an-active-session#Stopping an A: From the history page, click the stop button on any active session. This sends a stop request to the backend, which terminates the ECS task and deletes the Cloudflare DNS record.
data.cost-tracking#Cost Tracking:
Session cost is calculated from the actual Fargate vCPU and memory used by the container, read at stop time:
cost = (vcpu × vcpu_rate × hours) + (memory_gb × memory_rate × hours)
The rates are configured via environment variables:
Variable	Default	Description	FARGATE_VCPU_PER_HOUR_USD	0.04048	Cost per vCPU-hour	FARGATE_MEMORY_GB_PER_HOUR_USD	0.004445	Cost per GB-hour	FARGATE_SPOT_DISCOUNT	0.70	Multiplier applied to Spot sessions (30% discount)	
Costs are estimates. Your actual AWS bill may differ due to data transfer, ECR storage, and other charges not tracked here.
data.filtering-and-search#Filtering and Searc:
The history page supports filtering by:
Date range
Browser/OS image
Session status
Tags
Case
data.viewing-traffic-logs#Viewing Traffic Log: If network logging was enabled for a session, a traffic log is available in the session detail view. Click a session row to open the detail panel, then expand the Network Logs section.

data: Workspaces are the top-level organizational unit in OvB. All sessions, cases, and settings belong to a workspace.
data.types-of-workspaces#Types of Workspaces:
Type	Description	Personal	Created automatically when a user signs up. Private to the user.	Team	Shared workspaces with multiple members. Requires admin permission to enable.	
Whether personal or team workspaces can be created is controlled by the admin.
data.roles#Roles: Role	Permissions	owner	Full control: manage members, settings, and sessions	admin	Manage members and settings; cannot transfer ownership	member	Launch sessions, view history, manage their own cases
data.creating-a-workspace#Creating a Workspac:
If workspace creation is enabled by the admin:
Go to Workspaces in the sidebar
Click Create workspace
Enter a name and confirm
The creator becomes the workspace owner.
data.managing-members#Managing Members:
Workspace owners and admins can:
Invite users by email or username
Change a member's role
Remove members
data.workspace-settings#Workspace Settings:
Each workspace has independent settings that can override the global defaults:
Setting	Description	Browser allowlist	Restrict which browsers/OS images members can use	Idle timeout	Override the default session idle timeout	Concurrent session limit	Maximum simultaneous open sessions per user	Network logging	Enable or disable traffic logging for this workspace	File protection	Enable or disable encrypted file downloads for this workspace	
Workspace-level settings take precedence over global defaults (SiteSettings) when set.
data.switching-workspaces#Switching Workspace: Use the workspace switcher in the sidebar to move between workspaces. Sessions and cases are scoped to the workspace they were created in.
